name: Inclusion–Exclusion — Counting Without Double-Counting
whatIsIt: The INCLUSION–EXCLUSION principle counts a union without counting overlaps twice. For two sets: |A ∪ B| = |A| + |B| − |A ∩ B|. For three: |A ∪ B ∪ C| = |A| + |B| + |C| − |A∩B| − |A∩C| − |B∩C| + |A∩B∩C|. You ADD singles, SUBTRACT pairs, ADD back triples — alternating signs so every element is counted exactly once.
whyNeeded: Overlapping groups are everywhere (students taking multiple subjects, numbers divisible by several factors). Inclusion–exclusion gives the exact count without missing or double-counting — a core Olympiad combinatorics tool.
realLifeUses: Counting students who take at least one of maths, science, art
Counting numbers up to N divisible by 2, 3, or 5
Survey data where people picked more than one option

vocabulary.meaning: Everything in A or B (or both).
Only what is in both A and B (the overlap).
Add singles, subtract overlaps, add back triple overlaps.

story.title: The Club Sign-up
story.text: 30 students: 18 joined Music, 15 joined Dance, and 8 joined BOTH. 'How many joined at least one?' asks Meera. Teacher warns, '18 + 15 = 33 is too big — the 8 who did both got counted twice!' So |Music ∪ Dance| = 18 + 15 − 8 = 25. 'Subtract the overlap once,' she says. Meera nods: '25 joined at least one — no double-counting.'
story.extractedProblem: 18 in Music, 15 in Dance, 8 in both. How many joined at least one?

visual.caption: Inclusion–exclusion: |A ∪ B| = |A| + |B| − |A ∩ B|. The overlap is subtracted once so nobody is counted twice.

standardMethod.summary: To count a union, add the sizes of each set, subtract every pairwise overlap, then add back the triple overlap (for three sets). The signs alternate: + singles, − pairs, + triple.
steps: Add the sizes of the individual sets.
Subtract each pairwise intersection (overlap counted twice).
For three sets, add back the triple intersection (removed too many times).
The result counts each element exactly once.

workedExamples.problem: 18 in Music, 15 in Dance, 8 in both. How many in at least one?
Up to 100, how many are divisible by 2 or 5? (÷2: 50, ÷5: 20, ÷10: 10)

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding |A| + |B| and forgetting to subtract the overlap.
commonMistakes.fix: Subtract |A ∩ B| once — the overlap was counted in BOTH sets.

commonMistakes.mistake: For three sets, forgetting to add the triple intersection back.
commonMistakes.fix: Signs alternate: + singles, − pairs, + triple. Add |A∩B∩C| back at the end.

medium.q: 18 in Music, 15 in Dance, 8 in both. At least one = ?

questions.q: 20 in A, 12 in B, 5 in both. At least one?
For three sets, the triple intersection is…

teachBackPrompt: Use the club example with a grown-up: 18 in Music, 15 in Dance, 8 in both. Show why the answer is 18 + 15 − 8 = 25, not 33 — the 8 who did both were counted twice, so subtract the overlap once.

revisionCard.summary: Inclusion–exclusion: |A∪B| = |A| + |B| − |A∩B|. Three sets: add singles, subtract the three pairs, add back the triple. Signs alternate + − +. Use for overlapping groups and divisibility counts so nothing is double-counted.

rememberIt.hook: Add singles, subtract overlaps, add back the triple

studentQuestions.q: What if there are four sets?
studentQuestions.a: The pattern continues: add singles, subtract pairs, add triples, subtract the quadruple — signs keep alternating.

studentQuestions.q: How does this help with divisibility?
studentQuestions.a: To count numbers divisible by 2 or 3 up to N, add ⌊N/2⌋ and ⌊N/3⌋ then subtract ⌊N/6⌋ (divisible by both) so the overlap isn't double-counted.

formulas.name: Two sets
formulas.whenToUse: Counting a union of two groups.
